perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party agrees that it shall protect the Confidential Informatio n disclosed pursuant to the provisions of this Agreement using the same standard of care that it applies to safeguard its own proprietary, secret or Confidential Information but no less than a reasonable standard of care, and that the Confidential Informat ion shall be stored and handled in such a way as to prevent any unauthorized disclosure thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party agrees that it shall endeavor to protect the Confidential Information disclosed pursuant to the provisions of this Agreement using a standard of care it deems appropriate, and that the Confidential Information shall be stored and handled in a manner it considers suitable to prevent unauthorized disclosure thereof.
perturbation.explanation: The modification replaces 'shall protect...using the same standard of care that it applies to safeguard its own...but no less than a reasonable standard of care' with 'shall endeavor to protect...using a standard of care it deems appropriate'. This weakens the obligation and introduces subjectivity, potentially allowing the Receiving Party to provide inadequate protection. Replacing 'in such a way as to prevent any unauthorized disclosure thereof' with 'in a manner it considers suitable to prevent unauthorized disclosure thereof' further reduces the accountability for preventing disclosures.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Protection of Personal Information Act, 2013 (South Africa)
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 19 of POPIA mandates specific security safeguards for personal information, requiring organizations to implement reasonable technical and organizational measures to prevent loss, damage, or unauthorized access. The modified text, by allowing the Receiving Party to use a standard of care they deem 'appropriate' and a storage method they consider 'suitable,' may fall short of the 'reasonable' and defined measures required by POPIA, creating non-compliance.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Section 19 Security measures on integrity and confidentiality of personal information - POPIA Skip to content Search for: A responsible party must secure the integrity and confidentiality of personal information in its possession or under its control by taking appropriate, reasonable technical and organisational measures to prevent— loss of, damage to or unauthorised destruction of personal information; and unlawful access to or processing of personal information. In order to give effect to subsection (1), the responsible party must take reasonable measures to— identify all reasonably foreseeable internal and external risks to personal information in its possession or under its control; establish and maintain appropriate safeguards against the risks identified; regularly verify that the safeguards are effectively implemented; and ensure that the safeguards are continually updated in response to new risks or deficiencies in previously implemented safeguards. The responsible party must have due regard to generally accepted information security practices and procedures which may apply to it generally or be required in terms of specific industry or professional rules and regulations. We use cookies to ensure that we give you the best experience on our website. Are you happy for us to use cookies? Ok No Cookie policy
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to protect confidential information, potentially leading to non-compliance with POPIA. The original text mandates protection 'using the same standard of care...but no less than a reasonable standard of care,' while the changed text allows the Receiving Party to use a standard of care it 'deems appropriate.' This introduces subjectivity. The scraped law snippet 2 states a 'responsible party must secure the integrity and confidentiality of personal information...by taking appropriate, reasonable technical and organisational measures.' By allowing the Receiving Party to determine what is 'appropriate' and 'suitable,' the changed text fails to ensure 'reasonable' measures are taken, thus contradicting POPIA.

perturbation.original_text: The Counterparty must inform Eskom i n writing of any existing or potential conflict of interest with the interests of Eskom pursuant to this Project . Where any potential or actual conflict of interests exists, the prospective f irm shall immediately notify Eskom thereof in writing. Eskom at its sole discretion may elect whether the Counterparty can continue with the Project fully or partially and to the extent the Counterparty cannot continue with the Project it will renounce any benefits it may have obtained had it completed the Project and will be paid only for work completed. The omission of such information or the making of any false statements or representations shall entitle Eskom to terminate all contracts with the Counterparty regarding the Project and to invoke all remedies available to it in law .
perturbation.changed_text: The Counterparty should consider informing Eskom in writing of any significant conflict of interest with the interests of Eskom pursuant to this Project. Where a conflict of interests arguably exists, the prospective firm might notify Eskom thereof. Eskom, in its opinion, may consider whether the Counterparty can continue with the Project, and to the extent the Counterparty cannot continue with the Project, it will attempt to renounce any benefits it may have obtained and will be compensated for work completed. The omission of such information may allow Eskom to consider terminating contracts.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing "must inform" with "should consider informing" weakens the mandatory obligation to disclose conflicts of interest. Changing "shall immediately notify" to "might notify" further reduces the requirement to report conflicts. The phrase "significant" is ambiguous. Changing "Eskom at its sole discretion may elect" to "Eskom, in its opinion, may consider" makes Eskom's decision-making process more subjective and less definitive. Replacing "shall entitle Eskom to terminate all contracts" with "may allow Eskom to consider terminating contracts" makes the consequence of non-disclosure less certain.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Prevention and Combating of Corrupt Activities Act, 2004 (South Africa)
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 12 of the Act places a duty on certain persons to report corrupt transactions. By weakening the obligation to disclose conflicts of interest, the amended text could impede the reporting of corrupt activities that might be revealed through such disclosures. The original text provides a more robust framework for identifying and addressing potential corruption by mandating disclosure and enabling Eskom to take decisive action. The ambiguous language reduces the effectiveness of this framework.

perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly weakens the obligation to disclose conflicts of interest, moving from mandatory language ('must inform,' 'shall immediately notify') to discretionary language ('should consider informing,' 'might notify'). This undermines the original contract's intent to ensure transparency and accountability, potentially obstructing the identification and management of conflicts that could compromise the project. The Law Explanation notes this weakens the reporting of corrupt transactions. This falls under the Ambiguities Legal Contradiction definition, where the introduction of ambiguous obligations makes it difficult to enforce the original intent.

perturbation.original_text: The Disclosing Party may, at any time, and in its sole discretion request the Recei ving Party to return any material and/or data in whatever form containing, pertaining to or relating to Confidential Information disclosed pursuant to the terms of this Agreement and may, in addition request the Receiving Party to furnish a written stateme nt to the effect that, upon such return, the Receiving Party has not retained in its possession, or under its control, either directly or indirectly, any such material and/or data.
perturbation.changed_text: The Disclosing Party may, if it seems reasonable, request the Receiving Party to return accessible material pertaining to Confidential Information, and may also request a statement confirming the return of readily available material.
perturbation.explanation: The phrases "at any time, and in its sole discretion" are replaced by "if it seems reasonable," introducing a subjective element to the Disclosing Party's right to request the return of confidential information. The term "accessible material" narrows the scope of what needs to be returned, implying that certain difficult-to-access data may be exempt. Similarly, the phrase "readily available material" further limits the scope of the confirmation statement, potentially allowing the Receiving Party to retain confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Electronic Communications and Transactions Act, 2002 (South Africa)
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 84 of the Act deals with the protection of personal information and requires reasonable security measures to prevent unauthorized access or disclosure. By allowing for subjective interpretation of the return process and narrowing the scope of what information must be returned or confirmed as returned, the amendment undermines the security and control of confidential electronic data, potentially violating the requirement for reasonable security measures.
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces the Disclosing Party's right to request return of confidential information "at any time, and in its sole discretion" with "if it seems reasonable," introducing a subjective element. The scope of material to be returned is narrowed from "any material and/or data in whatever form" to "accessible material." These changes undermine the security and control of confidential data, potentially violating requirements for reasonable security measures. The Act requires reasonable security measures to prevent unauthorized access or disclosure, and by allowing a subjective interpretation of the return process and narrowing the scope of information to be returned, the amended contract weakens the protection of confidential information. Although Scraped Law Snippet 2 provides no content, GovInfo hints at the presence of regulatory requirements. This coupled with the 'Law Explanation' suggests a weakening of legally required security measures, thus resulting in a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party represents that it has authority to enter into this Agreement and to do all things necessary to procure the fulfillment of its obligations in terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party believes it has the authority to enter into this Agreement and will attempt to do what it considers necessary to fulfill its obligations.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "represents that it has authority" to "believes it has the authority" weakens the assurance of legal capacity and could undermine the contract's validity if a party later lacks the required authority. Replacing "to do all things necessary" with "will attempt to do what it considers necessary" introduces subjectivity and reduces the commitment to fulfill obligations, potentially allowing a party to avoid responsibilities based on their own interpretation of what is necessary.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Contract Law Principles in South Africa (General Principles of Contract)
perturbation.law_explanation: South African contract law requires that parties have the legal capacity (authority) to enter into an agreement and that there is a clear intention to be bound by the terms. The modified text's weakening of the representation of authority and the commitment to fulfilling obligations creates ambiguity about whether these fundamental requirements for a valid contract are met, potentially rendering the agreement unenforceable. The alteration introduces an element of uncertainty and reduces the level of commitment.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding the parties' authority and commitment to fulfilling their obligations, which weakens the agreement's enforceability. The original text provides a representation that each party 'has authority' and is committed 'to do all things necessary'. The changed text replaces this with 'believes it has the authority' and 'will attempt to do what it considers necessary', introducing a subjective standard. This shift creates uncertainty and weakens the assurance that the parties are legally bound, undermining the contract's validity and potentially rendering it unenforceable as stated in the Law Explanation.
